bytes.topic: Architecture & Structure — feature-based folder structure, colocation, barrel exports
bytes.title: React Feature Folders: Colocation Over Convention
bytes.body:
Grouping files by type (`components/`, `hooks/`, `utils/`) breaks down fast as apps grow — you end up chasing imports across the tree for a single feature. Feature-based folders flip this: every file a feature needs lives together. A `UserProfile` feature owns its component, hook, types, and tests in one directory. Colocation means the `useUserProfile` hook is only ever imported by `UserProfile` — it's never accidentally shared.

Barrel exports (`index.ts`) are the public API of each feature. Only what's explicitly re-exported is considered stable. Internal helpers stay unexported, enforcing a clear boundary. This makes refactoring safe: rename a file inside `features/user-profile/` and nothing outside breaks as long as the barrel contract holds.

Avoid deep barrel chains (barrel importing barrels) — they cause Webpack/Turbopack to pull in entire feature trees during tree-shaking analysis, inflating cold-start times. Keep barrels shallow: one level per feature boundary.

bytes.topic: Component Design — single responsibility, 150-line rule, presentation vs logic separation
bytes.title: React Component Design: Split at the Seam, Not Arbitrarily
bytes.body:
Every React component has two jobs fighting for space: fetching/transforming data and rendering UI. When they share a file, you get components that are hard to test, reuse, or reason about.

The split is simple: container components own state, effects, and data logic — they pass everything down as props. Presentational components are pure functions of props, no hooks beyond `useMemo`/`useCallback` for perf.

The 150-line rule is a smell detector, not a hard limit. A 200-line pure JSX component is fine. A 60-line component mixing `useEffect`, `fetch`, business logic, and render is already too coupled.

The real test: can you render your component in a Storybook story with just props? If you need to mock `fetch`, wrap it in a provider, or stub a context, your presentational layer has leaked logic.

Custom hooks are the extraction point. `useUserProfile()` returns `{ user, isLoading, error }` — the component consumes it. Now you can unit test the hook with `renderHook` and snapshot test the UI independently.

bytes.topic: State Management — local vs lifted vs context vs external store, when to use each
bytes.title: React State Placement: Local, Lifted, Context, or External Store?
bytes.body: Pick the narrowest scope that works. useState stays local when only one component needs the value — form inputs, toggle states, hover flags. Lift state to the nearest common ancestor when siblings need to share it; pass it down as props. Don't reach for Context to avoid prop drilling unless the data is truly global (theme, auth, locale) — Context re-renders every consumer on every change, so it's expensive for high-frequency updates. For server cache, async state, or cross-cutting UI state that changes often (cart, filters, optimistic updates), use an external store: Zustand for minimal boilerplate, Redux Toolkit when you need DevTools and middleware, Jotai or Recoil for fine-grained atom-level subscriptions that skip full-tree renders. The signal to upgrade: you're passing props more than two levels deep, or you're adding useEffect to sync two useState calls. That's the smell — not the proof — that you need to lift or externalize. Co-locate state with the component that owns it; only promote when forced.

bytes.topic: Performance — memoization (useMemo/useCallback/computed), when it helps vs hurts
bytes.title: React useMemo & useCallback: Stop Memoizing Everything
bytes.body:
Memoization in React isn't free — it has CPU cost for the comparison and memory cost for caching the previous value. The question isn't 'can I memoize this?' but 'is the recalculation more expensive than the comparison?'

useMemo is worth it for referentially stable values passed to memo()-wrapped children, or for genuinely expensive computations (sorting large arrays, heavy transforms). Wrapping a simple string concat or arithmetic in useMemo adds overhead, not removes it.

useCallback only pays off when the function is a dependency in another hook (useEffect, useMemo) or is passed as a prop to a memoized child. A bare event handler on a div doesn't benefit — React's reconciler doesn't bail out based on function identity for host elements.

The real win: profile first with React DevTools Profiler. Look for components with high render counts and expensive subtrees. Memoize at the boundary — the component receiving the prop — using React.memo(), then stabilize the prop with useMemo/useCallback upstream. Memoizing without React.memo() on the child is wasted work.

bytes.topic: Data Fetching — server state vs client state, TanStack Query patterns
bytes.title: Server State vs Client State in React — Stop Managing Both the Same Way
bytes.body:
Most React apps over-use useState for data that doesn't belong there. Server state (fetched from an API) is fundamentally different from client state (UI flags, form inputs): it's async, can go stale, is shared across components, and needs cache invalidation. Stuffing it into useState forces you to manually handle loading, error, and refetch logic everywhere.

TanStack Query treats server state as a first-class citizen. useQuery caches by queryKey, deduplicates in-flight requests, and revalidates on window focus automatically. useMutation pairs with queryClient.invalidateQueries() to keep the cache fresh after writes — no manual setState juggling.

Keep useState strictly for ephemeral UI state: modal open/closed, selected tab, optimistic toggles. If the data lives on a server, it belongs in TanStack Query. This boundary also unlocks devtools visibility into cache state, retries, and background refetches — invaluable for debugging race conditions that useState-based fetching silently swallows.

bytes.topic: Forms — controlled vs uncontrolled, React Hook Form + Zod pattern
bytes.title: Stop Writing Controlled Inputs — React Hook Form + Zod Is the Pattern
bytes.body:
Controlled inputs (`useState` per field) re-render on every keystroke and bloat component state. Uncontrolled inputs (ref-based) avoid re-renders but lose validation ergonomics. React Hook Form threads the needle: it registers inputs via refs under the hood, so your component stays uncontrolled and fast, while `useForm` gives you a single `errors` object and a type-safe `handleSubmit`.

Pair it with Zod via `@hookform/resolvers/zod` and you get schema-driven validation that colocates your types with your rules. Define a `z.object()` schema, infer the TypeScript type from it with `z.infer`, and pass the resolver to `useForm`. Now your form input types, validation rules, and submit handler payload are all derived from one source of truth — no manual type assertions, no duplicated error messages.

bytes.topic: TypeScript — prop typing, discriminated unions for variants, no any
bytes.title: React Discriminated Unions: Type-Safe Component Variants Without `any`
bytes.body:
Typing React props with `any` silently breaks refactoring — the compiler can't catch mismatched variant combinations. Discriminated unions fix this by adding a literal `type` field that narrows the type inside each branch. When you render a `<Button variant="primary" />`, TypeScript now knows exactly which props are valid for that variant — no casting, no guessing.

The pattern shines when variants need mutually exclusive props. A `LinkButton` needs `href`, a plain `Button` needs `onClick` — putting both optional on one interface lets invalid combinations compile silently. With a discriminated union, the compiler enforces the contract at the call site.

Use `React.ComponentPropsWithoutRef` to spread native element props safely without losing the discrimination. Avoid intersecting with `{ [key: string]: any }` — it defeats the entire model. If a prop legitimately accepts multiple shapes, reach for generics or explicit overloads instead. The goal is making illegal states unrepresentable inside JSX, not just at runtime.

bytes.topic: Error Handling — error boundaries per route, handle loading/error/empty states
bytes.title: React Route-Level Error Boundaries — Isolate Failures Without Crashing the App
bytes.body:
Wrapping your entire app in a single ErrorBoundary is a blunt instrument — one bad route tears down the whole UI. Instead, colocate boundaries at the route level so failures stay contained. In React Router v6, pair each lazy-loaded route with its own ErrorBoundary using the errorElement prop, and handle the three states — loading, error, empty — directly inside the component using useRouteError, Suspense, and conditional rendering.

For data fetching, React Query makes this pattern clean: isLoading, isError, and isEmpty are first-class states you branch on explicitly. Avoid generic catch-all fallbacks; render actionable UI — a retry button, a skeleton, or a contextual empty state — instead of a blank screen.

A critical detail: ErrorBoundary only catches render-phase errors, not async ones. Wrap async throws in state so they propagate through render. Use react-error-boundary's useErrorBoundary() hook to manually trigger the boundary from event handlers or effects without restructuring your component tree.

bytes.topic: Testing — test behaviour not implementation, Testing Library philosophy
bytes.title: React Testing Library: Query by Role, Not by Class
bytes.body: Most React test bugs come from testing implementation details — querying by class names, checking internal state, or asserting on component structure. When you refactor, those tests break even though the UI still works correctly. Testing Library's philosophy flips this: query the DOM the way a user or assistive technology would. Use `getByRole('button', { name: /submit/i })` instead of `getByTestId('submit-btn')`. Use `getByLabelText` for inputs instead of `querySelector`. Fire events with `userEvent.click()` instead of manually invoking handlers. Assert on what the user sees — visible text, ARIA roles, disabled states — not on React's internal tree. This makes your tests resilient to refactors: you can swap a `<div onClick>` for a `<button>`, rename CSS classes, or lift state up, and the tests stay green. The rule of thumb: if your test would still pass after completely rewriting the component internals, it's testing the right thing.

bytes.topic: Security — dangerouslySetInnerHTML, token storage, input sanitization
bytes.title: React Security Traps: XSS, Token Storage, and Sanitization
bytes.body: React's JSX auto-escapes strings, but three patterns break that guarantee. First, `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` bypasses React's escaping entirely — never pass unsanitized user content to it. Use DOMPurify's `sanitize()` before setting innerHTML, or better yet, render plain text via JSX. Second, storing JWTs in `localStorage` exposes them to any XSS payload on your domain — use `httpOnly` cookies issued by your backend instead; they're inaccessible to JavaScript. Third, controlled inputs don't sanitize — they just bind values. If you're feeding user input into a `dangerouslySetInnerHTML`, a query string, or an `eval`-adjacent API, sanitize explicitly. For rich text, DOMPurify is the standard; configure it with a strict `ALLOWED_TAGS` allowlist rather than relying on defaults. React's security model assumes you own the content pipeline — the moment user-generated strings touch raw DOM APIs or your token store, that assumption breaks. Treat every `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` usage as a code smell requiring a peer review sign-off.

bytes.topic: Code Quality — ESLint react-hooks plugin, naming conventions, 50-line rule
bytes.title: React Code Quality: eslint-plugin-react-hooks, Naming Rules, and the 50-Line Limit
bytes.body:
The `eslint-plugin-react-hooks` package enforces two non-negotiable rules: `rules-of-hooks` (no conditional hook calls) and `exhaustive-deps` (no missing dependencies in `useEffect`, `useMemo`, `useCallback`). Ignoring `exhaustive-deps` warnings is how stale closure bugs are born — the linter is almost always right.

Naming matters more in React than in most frameworks. Custom hooks must start with `use` — this isn't stylistic, it's what enables the linter to track hook call order. Components must be PascalCase; lowercase components are treated as DOM elements by JSX. Handler props should be `onX`, the handlers themselves `handleX`.

The 50-line rule applies at the component level: if your JSX plus logic exceeds ~50 lines, you have an implicit second responsibility. Extract it. A `UserCard` that fetches, formats, and renders is three components pretending to be one. Split into a data hook (`useUserCard`), a presenter (`UserCardView`), and a thin connector. Each becomes independently testable and the linter has less surface to misread.

bytes.topic: Accessibility — semantic HTML first, focus management, ARIA only when HTML falls short
bytes.title: React Accessibility: Semantic HTML First, ARIA as a Last Resort
bytes.body:
Most React accessibility bugs stem from using `<div onClick>` where a `<button>` belongs. Semantic HTML gives you keyboard support, focus management, and screen reader announcements for free — no extra code. Reach for ARIA only when native elements genuinely can't model your UI.

For focus management, use `useRef` + `.focus()` after state transitions — e.g., move focus to a modal heading when it opens, and back to the trigger when it closes. React 19's `<dialog>` support makes this even cleaner with native `showModal()` semantics.

When you do need ARIA, be surgical: `aria-live="polite"` for async status updates, `aria-expanded` on a toggle button that controls a collapsible, `role="status"` on toast containers. Never add `aria-label` to an element that already has visible text — it creates a mismatch for voice control users.

Lint-gate this with `eslint-plugin-jsx-a11y`. It catches missing `alt`, implicit roles, and invalid ARIA combos at author time, before they ship.

bytes.topic: Internationalization — never hardcode strings, react-i18next, Intl API for dates/numbers
bytes.title: React i18n: react-i18next + Intl API, No Hardcoded Strings
bytes.body:
Hardcoding strings in JSX is an i18n anti-pattern that creates refactor debt at scale. Use react-i18next's `useTranslation` hook to externalize all user-facing text into JSON namespaces. Wrap your app in `I18nextProvider` and call `i18n.changeLanguage('fr')` to hot-swap locales without a remount.

For dates and numbers, skip third-party libraries — the native `Intl` API handles locale-aware formatting directly in React. Use `useMemo` to avoid recreating `Intl.DateTimeFormat` and `Intl.NumberFormat` instances on every render.

Key patterns: use `Trans` component for JSX-interpolated strings (e.g., links inside translations), never template-literal concatenate translated substrings, and always namespace translations (`t('common:save')`) to enable lazy-loading via `i18next-http-backend`. Co-locate locale JSON files per feature slice, not one monolithic `en.json`. For pluralization, lean on i18next's built-in plural suffixes (`_one`, `_other`) rather than manual ternaries in JSX.

bytes.topic: Web Vitals — LCP, CLS, INP and how component patterns affect each
bytes.title: Web Vitals in React: How Suspense, Transitions, and Conditional Renders Break Your Scores
bytes.body: Each Core Web Vital has a React-specific failure mode. LCP tanks when you wrap above-the-fold content in a Suspense boundary — the fallback renders first, delaying the largest paint. Fix it by hoisting data fetching or using `loading="eager"` on hero images outside Suspense. CLS spikes when conditional renders swap in content without reserved space — a `{isLoaded && <Component />}` pattern collapses and expands layout. Use `min-height` or `aspect-ratio` on the container to hold space during the loading state. INP is React's trickiest: a heavy `onClick` that triggers a large state update blocks the main thread synchronously. `useTransition` marks the update as non-urgent, letting React yield to input events mid-render. Wrap expensive state transitions — filtering large lists, navigating between views — in `startTransition`. Avoid putting the transition around the user's direct feedback (e.g. button active state); only defer the expensive downstream work. Profile with React DevTools Profiler + Chrome's INP attribution breakdowns to pinpoint which component tree is the bottleneck.

bytes.topic: Memory Leaks — cleanup in useEffect/onUnmounted/onDestroy, AbortController, removeEventListener
bytes.title: React Memory Leaks — useEffect Cleanup, AbortController, and removeEventListener
bytes.body:
Every useEffect that sets state, subscribes to events, or fetches data is a leak waiting to happen if you skip the cleanup function. When a component unmounts before an async fetch resolves, calling setState on the dead component triggers React's "Can't perform a state update on an unmounted component" warning — and wastes memory.

Use AbortController to cancel in-flight fetch requests. Pass signal to fetch(), and in the cleanup return, call controller.abort(). React will call your cleanup on unmount and before re-running the effect on dependency changes.

For DOM event listeners added inside useEffect, always mirror every addEventListener with a removeEventListener in the cleanup — same reference, same options object shape. Arrow functions inline will never match, so extract the handler to a variable first.

Custom hooks that wrap subscriptions (WebSocket, ResizeObserver, IntersectionObserver) must return cleanup too — consumers shouldn't need to know the internals. Returning the unsubscribe/disconnect call from useEffect is the contract React expects.

bytes.topic: Real-time & WebSockets — singleton connection, reconnection logic, optimistic UI on send
bytes.title: React WebSockets — Singleton Connection, Reconnect, and Optimistic UI
bytes.body: Mounting a WebSocket inside useEffect per-component creates redundant connections. Instead, create a singleton outside React and share it via context. Use a ref to hold the socket instance so re-renders don't trigger reconnects. For reconnection, track readyState in state and schedule exponential-backoff retries with setTimeout — clear the timer in the cleanup function to avoid stale closures. For optimistic UI on send, append the message to local state immediately before calling socket.send(). Assign a clientId to each pending message. On server ack, replace the pending entry using that id; on error, mark it failed or roll back. Avoid putting the raw WebSocket in useState — it's not serializable and triggers unnecessary re-renders. Instead, keep it in a ref and only put derived UI state (messages, status) in useState. This pattern gives you a stable connection, resilient reconnect loop, and instant UI feedback without waiting for the round-trip.

bytes.topic: Environment & Config — never hardcode URLs or keys, validate env vars at startup with Zod
bytes.title: Validate React Env Vars at Startup with Zod — Catch Misconfigs Before They Hit Users
bytes.body:
Hardcoding API URLs or keys in React components is a silent killer — it breaks across environments and leaks secrets into bundles. Vite exposes only `VITE_` prefixed vars to the client; Create React App uses `REACT_APP_`. Either way, a missing var silently becomes `undefined`, causing runtime failures deep in your app.

The fix: validate your env contract at module load time using Zod. Create a `src/env.ts` file that parses `import.meta.env` (Vite) or `process.env` (CRA) against a schema. If validation fails, throw immediately — crashing at startup is infinitely better than a cryptic fetch error in production.

This gives you type-safe env access throughout the app (`env.VITE_API_URL` instead of raw strings), IDE autocomplete, and a single source of truth. Pair it with `.env.example` committed to git and `.env.local` gitignored. For CI, set vars as pipeline secrets — never commit real values. This pattern scales cleanly from local dev to staging to prod without a single URL change in component code.

bytes.topic: API Layer — single HTTP client instance, interceptors for auth/retry, never raw fetch in components
bytes.title: React API Layer — One Axios Instance, Zero Raw Fetches in Components
bytes.body:
Scattering `fetch()` calls across components creates silent auth failures, inconsistent error handling, and retry logic duplicated everywhere. The fix: a single Axios instance exported from `lib/api.ts`, configured once with interceptors.

Attach a request interceptor to inject the Bearer token from your auth store (Zustand, Context, or Clerk's `getToken`). Attach a response interceptor to handle 401s — clear the session and redirect — and to normalize error shapes before they bubble to components. For retries, `axios-retry` plugs directly into the instance with exponential backoff.

Components never touch Axios directly. They call typed service functions (`getUser(id)`, `postComment(payload)`) that wrap the instance. React Query or SWR then wraps those service functions — giving you caching, deduplication, and loading states for free.

This boundary keeps components dumb about transport. Swapping Axios for `ky` or adding an auth provider change? One file changes. Token refresh logic lives in the interceptor, not scattered across `useEffect` hooks.

bytes.topic: Animation — CSS for simple states, Framer Motion for complex, prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.title: React Animation: CSS for States, Framer Motion for Sequences, Always Respect prefers-reduced-motion
bytes.body: Reach for CSS transitions first — they're zero-bundle-cost and handle 80% of UI state changes (hover, focus, open/closed). Apply them via className toggling or inline style, letting the GPU handle interpolation. Framer Motion earns its 43 kB when you need orchestrated sequences, layout animations, or shared-element transitions that CSS can't express cleanly. Use its `motion` components and `AnimatePresence` for mount/unmount choreography. The non-negotiable: always honour `prefers-reduced-motion`. In CSS use the `@media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce)` block to zero out transitions. In Framer Motion, read `useReducedMotion()` and swap animated variants for instant ones. Wire this once in a top-level hook and pass a `shouldAnimate` flag via context — every component benefits automatically. Never gate the feature itself, only the motion: the UI must still transition between states, just without decorative movement.

bytes.topic: Global UI Patterns — toast system at app root, modal management, confirmation dialogs as hooks
bytes.title: Global UI Patterns — Toast, Modals, and Confirm Dialogs as React Hooks
bytes.body:
Scattering toast calls and modal state across components is a maintenance trap. The cleaner pattern: mount a single <ToastProvider> and <ModalProvider> at your app root, backed by a React context holding a queue or stack. Expose imperative hooks — useToast(), useModal(), useConfirm() — so any component can trigger UI without prop-drilling or lifting state.

useConfirm() is the highest-leverage example. Instead of rendering a <ConfirmDialog open={...}> in every feature component, you call await confirm('Delete this byte?') and get back a boolean. Internally, the hook resolves a Promise when the user clicks OK or Cancel — same pattern as window.confirm(), but fully styled.

This keeps your feature components free of UI orchestration logic. The modal stack in context handles z-index layering, focus trapping, and ESC key dismissal centrally. Pair it with Radix UI's Dialog primitive and you get accessible stacking out of the box. One root, one source of truth, zero modal state scattered across the tree.

bytes.topic: Bundle & Asset Optimization — tree-shaking, lazy-load heavy components, analyze bundle
bytes.title: Slash Your React Bundle: Tree-Shaking, Lazy-Loading, and Analysis
bytes.body:
React apps bloat fast. Three levers to pull immediately:

**Tree-shaking** dies silently when you use barrel imports. Replace `import { Button } from '@mui/material'` with `import Button from '@mui/material/Button'` — Webpack/Vite can now statically analyze and drop unused exports.

**Lazy-loading** with `React.lazy()` and `Suspense` splits your bundle at component boundaries. Pair it with route-level splitting via React Router's `lazy` prop to defer loading entire feature chunks until navigation.

**Bundle analysis** is non-negotiable before shipping. Run `npx vite-bundle-visualizer` (Vite) or add `webpack-bundle-analyzer` to your config. Look for duplicate packages (two versions of `lodash`), unexpectedly large transitive dependencies, and un-split vendor chunks.

Quick wins: set `sideEffects: false` in your `package.json` for pure utility libs, use dynamic `import()` for chart libraries like Recharts or D3, and keep your vendor chunk under 200KB gzipped. Most React apps can shed 30–50% just from fixing barrel imports.

bytes.topic: Dev Tooling & Quality Gates — Prettier, Husky + lint-staged, tsc --noEmit in CI
bytes.title: Lock Down Your React Repo: Prettier, Husky, and tsc in CI
bytes.body:
Shipping broken types or inconsistently formatted JSX wastes review cycles. A three-layer quality gate catches issues before they hit CI or your teammates.

First, Prettier handles formatting so nobody debates brace style in reviews — configure it with `singleQuote: true` and `jsxSingleQuote: false` for idiomatic React output.

Second, Husky + lint-staged runs checks only on staged files, keeping the pre-commit hook fast. Wire it to run `prettier --write` and `eslint --fix` on `*.{ts,tsx}` files so every commit is clean without blocking your flow.

Third — the one most React projects skip — run `tsc --noEmit` in CI, not just locally. This catches broken prop types, missing generics in custom hooks, and incorrect `useRef<T>` typings that eslint won't surface. Pair it with `"strict": true` in tsconfig for full coverage.

The payoff: type errors stay out of PRs, formatting is never a comment, and broken component contracts get caught in 30 seconds instead of production.

bytes.topic: Monitoring in Production — Sentry for errors, Web Vitals reporting, source maps
bytes.title: Production Monitoring in React: Sentry, Web Vitals, and Source Maps
bytes.body:
Sentry's React SDK wraps your app in an ErrorBoundary that captures uncaught exceptions with full component stack traces — not just JS call stacks. Pair it with `Sentry.withProfiler(App)` to get render performance data tied to real errors.

For Web Vitals, use the `web-vitals` package with `reportWebVitals` from Create React App — or call `onCLS`, `onLCP`, `onINP` directly in Vite setups — and pipe results into Sentry via `Sentry.captureMessage` with `level: 'info'` and metric data attached.

Source maps are critical: without them, Sentry shows minified stack traces. Configure your bundler to upload maps to Sentry during CI using `@sentry/vite-plugin` or `@sentry/webpack-plugin`. Set `SENTRY_AUTH_TOKEN` in your pipeline, enable `sourceMaps: { hidden: true }` so maps don't ship to the browser, and Sentry will automatically de-minify traces server-side. Never skip `release` tagging — it ties errors to the exact deploy that introduced them.

bytes.topic: Reactivity Models — how React, Vue, Svelte, and Solid each decide what re-runs on state change
bytes.title: React Re-renders Everything: How Its Reactivity Differs From Vue, Svelte, and Solid
bytes.body:
React's reactivity is coarse-grained: calling setState schedules a full component re-render, then reconciles the virtual DOM tree against the previous snapshot. Every child re-renders unless explicitly guarded with React.memo(), useMemo(), or useCallback(). Vue takes a different path — Proxy-based fine-grained reactivity means only expressions that read a reactive property re-execute. Svelte goes further by eliminating the runtime entirely; its compiler marks dirty variables at build time and surgically patches the DOM. Solid uses signals — primitive reactive primitives where only the specific DOM nodes or derived computeds subscribed to a signal re-run, with zero VDOM diffing overhead.

For React developers, this means a single useState update at a parent can silently cascade through an entire subtree. That's acceptable for most UIs, but it's the root cause of why you reach for memo boundaries. The React Compiler (available in React 19) automates memoization insertion, moving React closer to Svelte's compile-time model — but the scheduled re-render cycle itself remains React's fundamental contract.
